description: Hegel's entire philosophy is a sustained engagement with and transformation of Kant. Where Kant drew a firm boundary between appearances and things-in-themselves, Hegel dissolved it — arguing that the thing-in-itself is not an unknowable remainder but is progressively revealed through the development of Geist (Spirit/Mind) in history. Hegel accepted Kant's insight that subject and object are not simply given but actively related, and radicalised it: the relation is itself the substance of reality, not a veil over it. The Hegelian dialectic — thesis, antithesis, synthesis — is in large part a critique of Kant's static, ahistorical categories.
source: Hegel, Phenomenology of Spirit (1807), Preface and Introduction; Science of Logic (1812-16)
